Free access to all sculptures; donations welcome at the gift shop.

map_keywords: North Dakota Heritage Center State Museum Bismarck
Fort Abraham Lincoln State Park Mandan

visits.text: Start at the North Dakota Heritage Center and State Museum on the capitol grounds - North Dakota's largest museum and one of the best free museums on the Northern Plains. The 2014 expansion doubled it to 127,000 square feet across four galleries. The paleontology hall holds a life-size T. rex cast, but the showstopper is Dakota - a mummified Edmontosaurus discovered in 1999 near Marmarth, ND, and housed at the Heritage Center since 2008. Dakota is one of only a dozen fully mummified dinosaurs known to science, preserving extensive fossilized skin intact. The Early Peoples gallery displays over 1,000 artifacts from the Mandan, Hidatsa, and Arikara nations. Free admission every day.

Drive 7 miles south to Fort Abraham Lincoln State Park, North Dakota's oldest state park, established in 1907 when Theodore Roosevelt signed over the land. Lt. Col. George Armstrong Custer commanded the 7th Cavalry here from 1873 until he left for the Little Bighorn campaign in May 1876. At its height the fort held 650 men across 78 buildings; all were dismantled by settlers after its 1891 abandonment. The reconstructed Custer House is furnished as it would have been when Custer and his wife Libbie lived there. The On-A-Slant Village - a reconstruction of a Mandan earthlodge settlement occupied from around 1575 until a smallpox epidemic in the 1780s - completes the picture of who lived here before the fort arrived. Paid entry.

Walk the Missouri River waterfront at Keelboat Park, where a full-size replica of the Lewis and Clark keelboat sits at the river's edge. The Missouri at Bismarck is wide, brown, and powerful - the highway of exploration and westward expansion for two centuries. A statue of Sakakawea faces west, the direction she guided the Corps of Discovery. The state capitol dome rises just behind you. Free access.

activities.text: Take the self-guided tour of the North Dakota State Capitol - a 19-story Art Deco tower completed in 1934, the tallest building in either Dakota. The observation deck on the 18th floor gives a 360-degree view over the Missouri River valley and the rolling farmland. The guided tour covers the legislative chambers, murals, and the memorial hall. Free admission; observation deck closes at 4:30pm on weekdays.

Spend the evening in Bismarck's downtown around Main Avenue, where Laughing Sun Brewing and several other local spots sit alongside restaurants serving Northern Plains comfort food. If this is the last night of the road trip, it's a good place to eat well and look back at the week: a thousand-mile arc through battlefields, badlands, dinosaur bones, and the two states where the American West still feels most like itself.

seo.h1_title: Montana East: Big Sky Country from Billings to Bismarck
seo.intro_paragraph: This 6-day drive from Billings to Bismarck follows I-90 and I-94 through the heart of the American West, stopping at Little Bighorn where Custer fell in 1876, at Makoshika State Park where T. rex bones keep eroding out of the ground, and at Medora where Theodore Roosevelt spent the grief-filled years that shaped his conservation legacy. The towns are small, the distances are real, and the sky never seems to end.
seo.seo_hook: Little Bighorn, T-Rex fossils, and Roosevelt's badlands on one 6-day drive from Billings to Bismarck.
featured_list: Billings: Pictograph Cave holds 106 rock paintings between 200 and 2,100 years old, excavated alongside 30,000 artifacts in the 1930s
Little Bighorn: 263 U.S. cavalrymen killed June 25-26, 1876 - both sides now marked with their own memorial stones on Last Stand Hill
Glendive: Makoshika State Park, Montana's largest at 11,538 acres, sits on the Hell Creek Formation where over 10 dinosaur species have been found
Medora: Theodore Roosevelt first arrived in 1883 to hunt bison and left owning a ranch - these badlands built the conservationist

places_taglines.US::billings: Montana's largest city at the foot of 80-million-year-old sandstone cliffs.
places_taglines.US::hardin: Gateway to Little Bighorn, where the 7th Cavalry's defeat still echoes across the prairie.
places_taglines.US::miles-city: Cattle country capital where the World Famous Bucking Horse Sale has run every May since 1951.
places_taglines.US::glendive: T. rex and Triceratops bones still eroding out of Montana's largest state park.
places_taglines.US::medora: The North Dakota badlands town that remade Theodore Roosevelt into a conservationist.
places_taglines.US::dickinson-enchanted-highway: Thirty-two miles of world-record scrap metal sculptures rising from the prairie.
places_taglines.US::bismarck: Missouri River capital where Custer mustered for Little Bighorn and a dinosaur mummy waits in the state museum.

seo_keywords: Montana to North Dakota road trip

highlights: Little Bighorn Battlefield: 150th anniversary special NPS programming June 25-27, 2026
Real Bird Little Bighorn Reenactment: 34th annual edition June 26-28, 2026 near Garryowen MT
Miles City Bucking Horse Sale: 75th edition May 14-17, 2026, nearly 20,000 attendees
Makoshika State Park: 11,538 acres of Hell Creek Formation badlands with active paleontology digs each summer
Time zone shift: Medora is Mountain Time, Bismarck is Central Time - adjust planning on day 6

itineraries.title: Idaho: Sawtooth Mountains, Sun Valley & Craters of the Moon
itineraries.subtitle: Alpine lakes, America's first ski resort, and the lava fields where Apollo astronauts trained
itineraries.notes: 9-day loop from Boise airport (BOI). Mountain roads dominate Days 2-5: Highway 21 between Idaho City and Stanley has avalanche risk in winter/spring - check Idaho 511 before driving. Stanley regularly records the coldest overnight temperatures in the contiguous U.S. - bring layers even in July. Shoshone Falls is best March-June before irrigation diversions reduce the flow. Craters of the Moon loop road closes November-May for snow.

visits.text: Grove Street in downtown Boise is a single block that packs in the largest Basque community in North America - an estimated 16,000 residents whose ancestors left the Pyrenees in the late 1800s to herd sheep across Idaho's mountains. The cornerstone is the Cyrus Jacobs-Uberuaga House, built in 1864 and Boise's oldest surviving brick building; it served as a Basque boarding house from 1910 to 1968 and is now part of the Basque Museum & Cultural Center. The 1914 Anduiza Fronton next door still hosts handball and pala. Bar Gernika, opened in 1991 in a former 1912 Chinese laundromat, is the place for a pintxo and a glass of txakoli. The block is free to walk; the museum charges a small fee and closes on Sundays.

Steel doors clang shut behind you at the Old Idaho Penitentiary, and the sandstone walls - quarried by the inmates themselves from nearby Table Rock starting in 1872 - close in. The prison ran for exactly 101 years, housing over 13,000 people, including Harry Orchard, who assassinated Governor Frank Steunenberg in 1905 with a bomb wired to his front gate. Back-to-back riots in 1971 and 1973 burned the chapel and dining hall; the place closed in December 1973 and was left exactly as it stood that day, calendars still on the walls. Summer guided tours run daily from 10 AM to 5 PM for a fee - go at opening to avoid the wait.

Between 8th and 9th Street downtown lies Freak Alley - the Northwest's largest open-air mural gallery, born in 2002 when local artist Colby Akers painted a single alley doorway and the neighboring businesses asked for more. The project has since swallowed an entire city block and a gravel parking lot, with contributions from more than 200 artists. A $500,000 city renovation in 2018 expanded the space. Every August the murals are painted over during a week-long event and the whole thing starts again. Free and open 24 hours.

activities.text: Rent a bike or lace up your shoes and head to the Boise River Greenbelt - 29 miles of paved path tracing both banks of the river through the heart of the city. The trail was born in 1967 from three donated parcels of land; the 1971 Greenbelt Ordinance forced new buildings to set back at least 70 feet from the riverbank, protecting the corridor you're on today. The stretch between Ann Morrison Park and Barber Park is the most scenic, with great blue herons working the shallows in the early morning. Bike rentals are available from several downtown outfitters.

The Basque Block alone gives you two dinner options: Leku Ona, a boutique hotel and restaurant opened in 2005 inside a historic boarding house, and Bar Gernika, where the beef tongue sandwich sells out by noon on Saturdays. For a broader choice, the 8th Street strip packs in everything from wood-fired pizza to craft cocktails within a few blocks. Book ahead for dinner in summer - Boise fills fast in July.

visits.text: Idaho City peaked in September 1863 with 6,267 residents - larger than Portland at the time and the biggest city in the entire Pacific Northwest. Gold had been discovered in the Boise Basin on August 2, 1862; within eight months, the area became the largest settled region on the Pacific coast. The town was first called Bannock City, renamed in late 1863 by the Idaho Territorial Legislature to avoid confusion with Bannack, Montana. Walk the old main street past original frontier-era buildings; the Boise Basin Museum, housed in a historic post office building, tells the whole story with artifacts and photographs. Free admission.

Boot Hill Cemetery is a short walk above town, and the names on the markers are a ledger of the gold rush: gunfights, mining accidents, diphtheria, freezing. An 1863 Kansas newspaper described Idaho City simultaneously as the next El Dorado and one of the most desolate places on earth. Beyond the cemetery, the surrounding foothills are still scarred by dredge tailing piles from operations that ran here until 1952 - ridges and furrows that look from the right angle like frozen waves.

Highway 21 northeast of Idaho City is the Ponderosa Pine Scenic Byway - and the name earns its keep. The road climbs through dense pine forest, follows the South Fork of the Payette River through a tightening canyon, and passes the marker for the ghost town of Sawtooth City (founded 1879, abandoned 1888, now a few collapsed foundations in the trees). Stop at the roadside viewpoints: this was once the busiest supply route in Idaho Territory, carrying freight by pack mule before the first wagon road opened in 1864.

activities.text: Head 22 miles northeast on Highway 21 to Kirkham Hot Springs, where thermal waterfalls pour directly off rocky cliffs into pools on the bank of the South Fork Payette River. Source water reaches 120-135°F; the pools cool to a comfortable 95-110°F depending on proximity to the river. The site sits at 4,000 feet in Boise National Forest and is day-use only (7 AM to 9 PM); bring $5 cash or an America the Beautiful interagency pass. Arrive before 9 AM on summer weekends - by mid-morning it's packed.

practical_info.location: 5 miles east of Lowman on Hwy 21, milepost 77
practical_info.note: Day use only, no camping on site

activities.text: The stretch of Highway 21 between Lowman and Stanley is 58 miles of mountain road that takes about 90 minutes with stops - and the stops are worth it. Galena Summit at 8,701 feet is the high point, with a pullout where the full Sawtooth Valley suddenly opens below. In winter and spring this road can close without notice due to avalanche risk; check Idaho 511 before leaving Idaho City.

practical_info.route: Hwy 21 north from Idaho City to Stanley
practical_info.note: Mountain road - check avalanche conditions in winter/spring

visits.text: Stanley has a permanent population of roughly 70 people but three designated national scenic byways converge here, and on summer weekends the population multiplies tenfold. The town sits at 6,260 feet in a bowl-shaped basin below the Sawtooths - 57 peaks over 10,000 feet filling the western sky. This bowl shape is also why Stanley records overnight lows below freezing on average 294 days a year and was measured as the coldest place in the contiguous U.S. more often than any other weather station between 1995 and 2015. The Stanley Historical Museum, housed in the 1933 Valley Creek Ranger Station, gives the historical context in 20 minutes.

Five miles south on Highway 75, Redfish Lake spreads out at 6,500 feet below the serrated granite of the Sawtooth range - one of the clearest alpine lakes in the American West, fed almost entirely by snowmelt. The Redfish Visitor Center and Gallery at the lake's edge is the oldest National Forest visitor center in the contiguous United States, built in the early 1960s. In late summer and fall, sockeye salmon complete a 900-mile migration from the Pacific to spawn here. The Redfish Lake Lodge runs a sunset wine-and-appetizer lake cruise in summer - reserve a seat in advance, it sells out.

In December 2017, the Central Idaho Dark Sky Reserve became the first of its kind in the United States - 1,416 square miles encompassing the entire Sawtooth NRA, from Ketchum to northwest of Stanley. The designation requires that area businesses and municipalities limit light pollution, which means that on clear moonless nights over Stanley, the Milky Way appears as a textured band with visible lanes of intergalactic dust. No equipment needed. Just get away from the streetlights.

activities.text: Join one of the Stanley-based outfitters for a half-day on the Salmon River. The Upper Salmon runs Class III and IV rapids through a canyon flanked by the Sawtooths; June brings the highest water (snowmelt-fueled Class IV), while July and August mellow to Class III. Sawtooth Adventure Company runs full-day guided paddle-raft and inflatable kayak trips for all ability levels. At 425 miles, the Salmon is one of the longest free-flowing rivers in the lower 48 - this stretch is its headwaters.

practical_info.best_months: june for high water Class IV, july-august for Class III
practical_info.book: Reserve at least a week ahead in summer

activities.text: Snake Pit Hot Spring sits at the edge of town and costs nothing - rock pools fed by a thermal spring, with a view of the valley and the Sawtooth peaks. A small herd of deer routinely wanders through at dusk. Several other springs are within a 30-minute drive on Highway 75 toward Ketchum; the Mountain Village Lodge in Stanley also has access to commercial hot spring pools if you want facilities and privacy.

map_keywords: Sawtooth Lake Trail Iron Creek Stanley

visits.text: Eleven miles west on Highway 21, the Iron Creek Trailhead is the launch point for the Sawtooth Lake Trail - arguably the best single day hike in the range. The route climbs 4.3 miles through open forest and then granite scree to Sawtooth Lake, the largest alpine lake in the Sawtooth Wilderness, sitting under the sheer face of Mount Regan. The Sawtooth Wilderness was established in 1972 and covers 217,000 acres; Thompson Peak at 10,751 feet is the highest point. Expect snow on the upper trail before mid-June. The parking area fills before 9 AM on summer weekends.

The 3-mile side road to Stanley Lake branches off Highway 21 and dead-ends at one of the most photographed lakes in the Sawtooths - a glacially carved basin directly below 9,860-foot McGown Peak, whose reflection dominates the surface on calm mornings. Motorized boats are limited here, so the water is quiet. This is also the spot, at mile 117 on Highway 21, where the Sawtooth peaks first come into view for drivers approaching from Boise - a good indicator of what's been visible behind you all day.

Thirty miles south of Stanley on Highway 75, the Galena Summit pullout at 8,701 feet gives the best single view on this entire route: the Sawtooth Valley unrolling northward below, the Salmon River as a thread of silver at the valley floor, and on clear days, a wall of peaks extending to the horizon in every direction. The overlook is named for Senator Frank Church and his wife Bethine - Church championed the 1980 Central Idaho Wilderness Act that protected much of what you're looking at.

activities.text: Rent a kayak or paddleboard at Redfish Lake and spend the morning on the water. The Bench Lakes Trail begins on the west shore and leads to four smaller alpine lakes in about 4 miles round trip. For something different, the Redfish Lake Corrals - operated by Mystic Saddle Ranch since 1969 - runs guided horseback rides through the Ponderosa pines along the lakeshore and into the wilderness above. Either way, plan to be back at the lake by late afternoon when the alpenglow hits.

Stanley's handful of restaurants punch well above their weight. The Stanley Baking Co. handles breakfast and lunch, with cinnamon rolls that are discussed with unusual seriousness by people who have driven three hours for them. The Stanley Supper Club handles dinner. After eating, the Stanley to Redfish Lake Trail - a 5-mile paved and gravel path built with Sawtooth Society grant funds - makes a good evening walk.

visits.text: The Sun Valley Lodge opened on December 21, 1936, built in seven months on the former 3,888-acre Brass Ranch for $1.5 million by Union Pacific Railroad chairman Averell Harriman - who also installed the world's first chairlift here, designed by engineer Jim Curran using banana-loading conveyor technology from tropical cargo ships. The four-story, X-shaped building looks today exactly as it did then: concrete poured inside rough-sawn forms and acid-stained to resemble wood. Walk the hallways - they're lined with black-and-white photographs of Clark Gable, Ingrid Bergman, and Marilyn Monroe. In suite 206, Hemingway finished writing For Whom the Bell Tolls in the fall of 1939.

About 1.5 miles northeast of the Lodge on Trail Creek Road, a bronze bust by sculptor Robert Berks marks the Hemingway Memorial. It was dedicated on July 21, 1966 - his birthday, five years after his death in nearby Ketchum. The inscription is a eulogy Hemingway himself wrote in 1939 for a friend killed in a hunting accident, ending: 'Now he will be part of them forever.' The road closes in deep winter. Trail Creek itself runs below the memorial, clear enough to see the bottom.

On North Main Street, Hemingway's grave in the Ketchum Cemetery sits between two large spruce trees under a flat granite slab - usually decorated with bottles of whiskey left by visitors. He moved to Ketchum permanently in 1959 and died here on July 2, 1961. Nearby, the free Wood River Museum of History and Culture (4th and Walnut, Tuesday-Saturday 11 AM-6 PM) has an exhibit with original artifacts and a vintage typewriter you can use. The museum also covers Sun Valley's ski history and the local mining era.

activities.text: The Pioneer Cabin Trail climbs 4.5 miles west of Ketchum to a stone cabin at 9,400 feet, built by Union Pacific in 1937 as a ski hut. Three thousand feet of elevation gain, mostly in the first two miles through shaded pine - the hard part is done early. From the ridge, the Pioneer Mountains open up in every direction. Allow 5-6 hours round trip; late June through September for best conditions. Dogs welcome off-leash.

The Pioneer Saloon on Main Street has been here since Hemingway's era and still displays one of his personal shotguns - a 1953 Winchester Model 21 he used bird hunting on nearby Silver Creek. The Ram restaurant opened in 1938 and is the oldest at the resort. For the full literary cocktail experience, the Sawtooth Club serves the original Hemingway daiquiri recipe brought back from the El Floridita bar in Havana.

map_keywords: Craters of the Moon National Monument Idaho
Inferno Cone lava Craters of the Moon
lava tube caves Craters Moon Idaho

visits.text: The black basalt at Craters of the Moon starts without warning - you're driving through sagebrush high desert and then the lava is simply there, covering everything. President Coolidge established the national monument on May 2, 1924, to preserve what he called 'unusual and weird volcanic formations.' The Great Rift - a 52-mile crack in the Earth's crust - produced 60 distinct lava flows from 15,000 to just 2,100 years ago, making this one of the youngest lava fields in North America. In August 1969, astronauts Eugene Cernan and Joe Engle trained here with NASA geologist Ted Foss; Cernan later walked on the Moon during Apollo 17, while Engle was originally assigned to that mission but replaced by geologist Harrison Schmitt. Admission is free. Start at the Robert Limbert Visitor Center.

Climb the Inferno Cone - a steep 0.8-mile round trip up an 800-foot cinder cone - for a panoramic view of the lava field, and on clear days, the Teton Range 100 miles east. Then pick up a free cave permit at the visitor center and head to the Cave Trail, where lava tubes descend into the rock. The tubes stay near freezing year-round regardless of outside temperature; bring two light sources and a jacket. They formed when lava surfaces hardened while molten rock kept flowing inside, then drained, leaving hollow tunnels up to 30 feet high.

The Devils Orchard trail (0.5 miles, paved, free) winds through a kipuka - an island of older terrain completely surrounded by newer lava, hosting some of the oldest juniper trees in Idaho. The spatter cones visible along the loop road are among the best examples anywhere: steep chimneys formed where gas-rich lava blobs welded together above the rift into towers that look, from a distance, like broken teeth.

activities.text: After dark, stay in the park. Craters of the Moon is a certified International Dark Sky Park, and at 5,000 feet with zero nearby light pollution, the Milky Way is not a faint smear but a structure - dense at the core, fading at the edges, with dark lanes of dust visible to the naked eye. Rangers run Star Parties with telescopes through summer. The Lava Flow Campground, set directly on the basalt inside the monument, must be reserved at Recreation.gov for July and August.

practical_info.camping: Lava Flow Campground - reserve at Recreation.gov
practical_info.best_nights: New moon nights; late September-October for clearest skies and cooler temperatures

activities.text: Nineteen miles east of the park, Arco is the closest town - and 18 miles beyond that, EBR-1 (Experimental Breeder Reactor No. 1) is a National Historic Landmark where, on July 17, 1955, Arco became the first town in the world powered by atomic energy. The original reactor, control room, and instruments are all intact. Self-guided tours are free from Memorial Day through Labor Day. Allow 45 minutes.

map_keywords: Perrine Bridge BASE jumping Twin Falls
Snake River Canyon Trail Twin Falls

visits.text: At the rim of the Snake River Canyon, Shoshone Falls drops 212 feet - taller than Niagara - across a horseshoe-shaped cliff nearly 1,000 feet wide. The canyon itself was carved in a matter of weeks during the Bonneville Flood roughly 14,000 years ago, when Lake Bonneville released 1,100 cubic miles of water in a catastrophic overflow. Thomas Moran, who had already painted Yellowstone, came here in 1900 for the Pan-American Exposition. See it in spring: by summer, irrigation diversions on the Snake can reduce the flow to a trickle. Admission is $5 per car from March 30 through September.

The I.B. Perrine Bridge is 1,500 feet long and its deck hangs 486 feet above the Snake River - the only man-made structure in the United States where BASE jumping is legal year-round without a permit. On most weekends, jumpers step off the east side and deploy their parachutes two seconds into a five-second freefall, drifting to the canyon floor. Evel Knievel's 1974 steam-rocket launch ramp is still visible on the south rim, 1.6 miles east of the bridge. The pedestrian walkway is free.

The Canyon Trail descends from the south rim to the Snake River floor and continues east along the water, passing under the bridge's arches. From the canyon bottom, you can rent a kayak and paddle upstream to Blue Heart Springs - a turquoise spring that emerges directly from the basalt wall and is only reachable by water. The canyon rim trail loop above is 3 miles and free.

activities.text: TandemBASE, based in Twin Falls, runs commercial tandem jumps off the Perrine Bridge - you're strapped to a qualified instructor and fall 486 feet in about five seconds before the parachute opens. Minimum age 18; fee applies. If that's a firm no, the canyon walls below the bridge have bolted sport climbing routes, and the visitor center at the south end of the bridge points you to current access.

practical_info.book: tandembase.com - reserve in advance, especially June-August

activities.text: Magic Valley - the agricultural region around Twin Falls - produces more than 70% of all commercial trout eaten in the United States, raised in spring-fed farms whose water comes out of the canyon walls at a constant 59°F year-round. Rock Creek Restaurant and Snake River Brewing both serve it well, alongside local dairy cheese and Magic Valley potatoes. The Thursday summer farmers' market on Shoshone Street (June through October) is one of the better ones in southern Idaho.

visits.text: The skull of a Hagerman Horse dominates the visitor center at Hagerman Fossil Beds - Equus simplicidens, Idaho's state fossil, the earliest known member of the genus Equus. Rancher Elmer Cook spotted bones eroding from the bluffs along the Snake River in 1928 and called the U.S. Geological Survey; the Smithsonian dug here from 1929 to 1934 and uncovered more than 200 individual horse specimens of all ages and both sexes, alongside mastodons, saber-toothed cats, and ground sloths, all 3 to 4 million years old. The fragile sandstone bluffs are restricted; the visitor center is the place to see the fossils. Free admission.

The Oregon Trail emigrants who passed through this valley in the 1840s and 1850s stopped and stared: springs poured straight out of the canyon walls by the dozens, cascading down basalt cliffs into the Snake River below. They were looking at overflow from the Snake River Plain Aquifer, recharged every year by snowmelt from the Rocky Mountains above. At Malad Gorge today, the Malad River drops through stairstep falls into the Devil's Washbowl then cuts a 250-foot gorge; the bridge crossing it gives you the best view. One park fee ($7) covers all six units of Thousand Springs State Park.

Ritter Island sits in the Snake River as a green anomaly - surrounded by water and canyon walls, with Lemon Falls cascading into view on all sides. In the early 20th century, Minnie Miller ran a state-of-the-art Guernsey dairy farm here; her original buildings are still standing. The Nature Conservancy bought the 385-acre property in 1986. Access is Thursday through Monday, Memorial Day through Labor Day; a rental house on the island is bookable year-round through Idaho State Parks.

activities.text: Hagerman's stretch of the Snake River runs Class I-III whitewater through a canyon whose walls are the same fossil-bearing bluffs you visited this morning. Several local outfitters run guided half-day trips with the fossils above you the whole time. For something quieter, the Billingsley Creek unit of Thousand Springs State Park has excellent fly-fishing - the creek is fed by constant-temperature springs and stocked with trout year-round.

Clear Springs Foods, headquartered just outside Hagerman, is one of the largest commercial trout producers in North America - so the fish at local restaurants is as fresh as it gets. The Hagerman Valley Inn handles dinner well. For breakfast, Bullets N' Brews is exactly what the name suggests: espresso and breakfast burritos alongside shelves of ammunition, which is more normal in rural Idaho than it sounds.

visits.text: The Idaho State Capitol on Jefferson Street was completed in 1920 and is the only state capitol in the United States heated by geothermal water, drawn from a 3,000-foot well directly beneath the building. The interior dome rises 208 feet and is lined with four types of marble, including local Boise Basin stone. Self-guided tours are free on weekdays - check capitol.idaho.gov for guided tour times if you want the full context.

Julia Davis Park on the south bank of the Boise River holds the Idaho State Museum (rebuilt and reopened 2018), the Boise Art Museum, and the Idaho Anne Frank Human Rights Memorial - a free outdoor installation of granite walls engraved with human rights quotations, set among cottonwoods along the river. Thomas Davis donated the land in 1907 in memory of his wife. The park sits between downtown and Boise State University, which means it's lively on weekdays and a genuine retreat on weekend mornings.

Climb the bell tower of the Boise Depot at 2603 W Eastover Terrace and you're at the second-tallest point in the city, with views over the foothills and on clear days, the Sawtooth peaks 100 miles northeast. Built by Union Pacific in 1925 in Spanish Colonial Revival style, this was the terminal where Harriman's express trains unloaded Hollywood celebrities bound for Sun Valley. It's now a free public space; the building itself is worth the visit for the tile work alone.

activities.text: Rent a tube at Barber Park and float 5.5 miles back through the cottonwood corridor of the Boise River Greenbelt to Ann Morrison Park - 2 to 3 hours depending on current, with great blue herons overhead and the city skyline in the background. The water comes from Lucky Peak Reservoir and runs cold; late June through August is the sweet spot. Tube rentals and shuttles back to Barber Park operate on summer weekends from the downstream parks.

practical_info.season: June through August when water levels and temperatures are suitable
practical_info.note: Tubes for rent at Barber Park; shuttle back to start required

activities.text: The loop closes on 8th Street, where Bittercreek Alehouse has been serving locally sourced Idaho food and regional craft beer since 1997. For a Basque send-off, Bar Gernika is open for lunch Tuesday through Saturday - the lamb sandwich and a Basque cider is the right order. Book a late-afternoon flight from BOI. Nine days and Idaho still has more to give.

seo.h1_title: Idaho: Sawtooth Mountains, Sun Valley & Craters of the Moon
seo.intro_paragraph: This 9-day loop from Boise covers three of Idaho's most distinct landscapes: the gold-rush ghost towns and alpine lakes of the Sawtooth Mountains, the birthplace of the modern chairlift at Sun Valley's 1936 Lodge, and the 750,000-acre lava fields where Apollo astronauts Eugene Cernan and Joe Engle trained in 1969. The route follows three designated national scenic byways through some of the least-crowded mountain terrain in the American West.
seo.seo_hook: Sawtooth alpine lakes, America's first ski resort, Apollo training grounds, and the highest waterfall in the West.
featured_list: Stanley: gateway to the Sawtooth Wilderness - 40 peaks over 10,000 feet and the U.S.'s first International Dark Sky Reserve (2017)
Sun Valley: America's first destination ski resort, opened December 21, 1936, and home to the world's first chairlift
Craters of the Moon: 60 lava flows from 15,000 to 2,100 years ago, where NASA trained Apollo astronauts in August 1969
Twin Falls: Shoshone Falls drops 212 feet - 45 feet higher than Niagara - above the only U.S. bridge where BASE jumping needs no permit

places_taglines.US::boise: Idaho's capital city with the largest Basque community in North America.
places_taglines.US::idaho-city: Gold rush boomtown that briefly outgrew Portland in 1863.
places_taglines.US::stanley: Population 70, but three scenic byways and the Sawtooth Wilderness converge here.
places_taglines.US::ketchum: Hemingway's last home, beside the resort he helped make famous.
places_taglines.US::arco: Closest town to Craters of the Moon and the world's first nuclear-powered city.
places_taglines.US::twin-falls: Shoshone Falls and the only bridge in the U.S. where BASE jumping is always legal.
places_taglines.US::hagerman: Fossil beds preserving 200+ species from 3 to 4 million years ago, including Idaho's state fossil.

seo_keywords: Craters of the Moon National Monument

highlights: Sawtooth Wilderness: 217,000 acres established 1972, 57 peaks over 10,000 feet, first U.S. International Dark Sky Reserve (2017)
Sun Valley Lodge: opened December 21, 1936, world's first chairlift, Hemingway completed For Whom the Bell Tolls in suite 206
Craters of the Moon: 60 lava flows from 15,000-2,100 years ago, Apollo 14 astronauts Cernan and Engle trained here in August 1969
Shoshone Falls: 212 feet tall and nearly 1,000 feet wide - 45 feet higher than Niagara, best in spring
Hagerman Fossil Beds: 200+ Hagerman Horse fossils uncovered 1929-1934 by the Smithsonian, 3-4 million years old

itineraries.source_url: https://www.cheyenne.org/, https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wyoming_State_Capitol, https://cfdrodeo.com/about-us/, https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wyoming_Territorial_Prison_State_Historic_Site, https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Vedauwoo, https://www.fs.usda.gov/wildflowers/regions/Rocky_Mountain/SnowyRangeByway/index.shtml, https://www.saratogachamber.info/play/mineral-hot-springs, https://wyominganglers.com/wyoming-trout-fishing-destinations/north-platte-saratoga-fly-fishing/, https://wyoparks.wyo.gov/index.php/places-to-go/sinks-canyon, https://www.climbersfestival.org/, https://wyoparks.wyo.gov/index.php/places-to-go/hot-springs, https://www.atlasobscura.com/places/the-wyoming-dinosaur-center-dig-sites, https://centerofthewest.org, https://cowboylifestylenetwork.com/cody-nite-rodeo-2025/, https://www.wyohistory.org/encyclopedia/medicine-wheel, https://www.fs.usda.gov/r02/bighorn/natural-resources/arch-cultural/medicine-wheel-medicine-mountain-national-historic, https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Irma_Hotel, https://wyoshpo.wyo.gov/index.php/programs/national-register/wyoming-listings/view-full-list/738-irma-hotel, https://historicwyoming.org/profiles/sheridaninn/, https://downtownsheridan.org/explore/walking-tour/kings-saddlery/, https://www.visitcasper.com/things-to-do/
itineraries.title: Wyoming Beyond Yellowstone: Cowboys, Hot Springs & Bighorn Medicine Wheel
itineraries.subtitle: The Wyoming that most visitors drive through without stopping - rodeo towns, sacred mountains, and the real American West
itineraries.notes: 8-day loop from Denver (DEN), starting and ending in Cheyenne (90 min north on I-25). Two intermediate stops: Rawlins (0.3 day, Day 4) and Casper (0.3 day, Day 9). Drive legs exceeding 2h but under 4h tolerated where the route is a designated scenic byway or mountain pass (Days 2→3 via Snowy Range Byway 2h20; Days 4→5 via US-287 plains 2h10; Days 7→8 via US-14A Bighorn Mountains 3h05). The US-14A Medicine Wheel Passage has grades exceeding 10% - not suitable for trailers or large motorhomes. Medicine Wheel access road opens July 1. Snowy Range Scenic Byway closes mid-October.

map_keywords: Cheyenne Frontier Days Old West Museum

visits.text: The Wyoming State Capitol's gold-leafed dome has been gilded five times since 1900 - from the north it floats above the city like something European. Inside, the first-floor rotunda has checkerboard marble floors and a cherry-wood staircase shipped from Ohio. The building was finished in 1890, the same year Wyoming became the 44th state, and the second-floor chamber is where delegates voted to enshrine women's suffrage in the state constitution - Wyoming women had held that right since 1869, five decades before the 19th Amendment. Self-guided tours are free, Monday through Friday during business hours.

Cheyenne was nothing but open plains until 1867, when Union Pacific drove its tracks west and the city appeared in a matter of months. That story is inside the Cheyenne Depot Museum, housed in the original 1886 railroad station - a National Historic Landmark. The second floor holds a wood-carved Big Boy locomotive model and a full-scale model railroad display; labels cite their sources like footnotes, which is unusual and worth slowing down for. Plan 90 minutes if you read everything.

The Cheyenne Frontier Days Old West Museum holds one of the largest collections of horse-drawn carriages in the country and thousands of rodeo artifacts tracing the event to its first day: September 23, 1897, when Union Pacific agent Frederick W. Angier organized a one-day cowboy roundup that drew 4,000 people. By year two, 20,000 showed up. Today it draws 300,000 visitors over 10 days and pays out more than $1 million in prize money. Worth 60-90 minutes even outside rodeo season.

activities.text: Scattered across downtown Cheyenne are dozens of 8-foot cowboy boots painted by local artists, each depicting a different slice of Wyoming history - the Big Boots art trail. Pick up a location map at the Depot Museum visitor kiosk, or call (307) 316-0067 for a cell phone audio tour where artists explain their own work. The main cluster near the historic district takes 45-60 minutes to cover, and you'll see more of the old town than any guided bus tour would show you.

practical_info.tip: Audio tour: (307) 316-0067, press boot number then #

activities.text: Curt Gowdy State Park sits 24 miles west in the foothills of the Laramie Mountains - granite outcrops, rainbow trout in Granite Springs Reservoir, pronghorn antelope grazing in sight of the trail, and over 35 miles of mountain biking. It's one of the few spots near Cheyenne where the city genuinely disappears. Day use fee applies.

practical_info.access: I-80 west then WY-210, fee applies for day use

map_keywords: Wyoming Territorial Prison State Historic Site Laramie
University of Wyoming Geological Museum Laramie

visits.text: Built in 1872 as one of the few federal prisons of its time, the Wyoming Territorial Prison State Historic Site has sandstone walls that still feel oppressive. Of the 44 prisoners taken in during the first two years, 11 escaped. The most famous resident was Butch Cassidy, held here from 1894 to 1896 for grand larceny. After the prison moved to Rawlins in 1901, the University of Wyoming used the building as a livestock experiment station until 1989 - a strange afterlife for a place this grim. The site sits on 197 acres with a nature trail along the Big Laramie River. Fee applies; open Memorial Day through Labor Day.

A full Apatosaurus skeleton greets you near the entrance of the University of Wyoming Geological Museum - one of the more complete sauropod specimens on display anywhere in the state. The collection covers Wyoming's rich fossil record including dinosaur trackways and Morrison Formation specimens. Free admission. Allow 45-60 minutes; the historic Romanesque Revival campus buildings are worth a walk afterward.

At I-80 Exit 329, sixteen miles east of Laramie, the landscape breaks open into something that looks structurally impossible: 1.4 billion-year-old Sherman Granite stacked in towers up to 500 feet high. The Arapaho called this place 'bito'o'wu' - earth-born - a name popularized by a University of Wyoming drama professor in 1922. Over 900 climbing routes thread the rock; non-climbers can walk the 3.1-mile Turtle Rock Loop through aspen groves with minimal elevation gain. Day use fee applies.

activities.text: WY-130 west from Laramie is the day's main event - the Snowy Range Scenic Byway rises to 10,847 feet at Libby Flats, the second-highest mountain pass in Wyoming, designated the second National Forest Scenic Byway in 1988. Lake Marie is the best stop: Medicine Bow Peak (12,013 feet) appears to grow straight out of the water. At Libby Flats, views extend south into Colorado. If you're staying in Laramie tonight, make it an out-and-back; otherwise it's tomorrow's route to Saratoga.

practical_info.warning: Closes mid-October; check for late-season snow on the high pass even in June

activities.text: Laramie's historic downtown repays an evening on foot - locally owned shops, colorful murals, and places like Range Leather Co. (handcrafted leather goods) and the Bent and Rusty (antiques and oddities). The town was born in 1868 when Union Pacific arrived; within months it had a population of several thousand. For dinner, try the Crowbar and Grill for hand-tossed pizza or Sweet Melissa for creative vegetarian dishes a block away.

to_next_leg.road_type: mountain pass (WY-130 Snowy Range Scenic Byway, max elevation 10,847 ft)

map_keywords: North Platte River fly fishing Saratoga

visits.text: In Saratoga's City Park on Walnut Street, a public mineral hot spring sits open 24 hours a day, 365 days a year, free of charge - the Hobo Hot Pool. It ranges from 106°F at its coolest end to 119°F at the 'Lobster Pot.' The current pool was discovered in 1917 after a flood eroded the banks of Spring Creek. Native Americans used the original springs here as neutral territory for centuries; the town was named Saratoga in 1884 after an Iroquois word meaning 'place of miraculous water in the rock.' Restrooms and changing facilities are on site.

The Hotel Wolf on Bridge Street has been operating since the late 19th century and remains the social hub of the North Platte Valley. The bar and restaurant serve some of the better steaks in the region. Sit down at lunch and listen to the tables around you - ranchers, outfitters, and visiting anglers talking about water levels and hatch conditions. This is what Saratoga actually is.

Five miles north of town on WY-130/230, the Saratoga National Fish Hatchery raises multiple trout species for federal and state hatcheries nationwide and runs a captive breeding program for the endangered Wyoming Toad. Free to visit; a short self-guided tour explains the operation. Context worth having: the North Platte at Saratoga holds over 4,000 catchable wild trout per mile and has not been stocked since 1979.

activities.text: A guided float trip on the Upper North Platte is the full-day option - 70 miles of blue-ribbon water holding over 4,000 wild trout per mile (browns and rainbows averaging 13-17 inches), unstocked since 1979. The section from Bennett Peak to Treasure Island to Saratoga runs through canyon scenery and cottonwood-lined banks. Mid-June to early August is peak season when spring runoff clears. Reserve at least a week in advance in summer.

practical_info.tip: Book at least one week in advance in summer

activities.text: Twenty minutes south of Saratoga lies Encampment, and from there Battle Highway (WY-70) heads west to 'Aspen Alley' - a half-mile stretch of dirt road where tall aspens arch completely overhead, glowing gold in late September. The Grand Encampment Museum covers the 1890s-1900s copper mining boom with over a dozen original buildings; free admission. A 2-3 hour loop that shows a completely different face of Carbon County.

practical_info.tip: Aspen Alley peaks late September-early October

visits.text: Pull off I-80 in Rawlins for the Wyoming Frontier Prison - Wyoming's first state penitentiary, 80 years in operation from 1901 to 1981, roughly 13,500 inmates including Big Nose George. Guided tours run through the dungeon, solitary confinement cells, and the gas chamber where 14 executions took place. One-hour tours run year-round. The building is on the National Register of Historic Places.

Step into the Carbon County Museum a few blocks away - free admission, 30,000 artifacts, and the only place in the world where you can see shoes custom-made from the skin of outlaw Big Nose George Parrot after his 1881 hanging. Exhibits also cover Thomas Edison's visit to the Rawlins area, where he reportedly found inspiration for his lightbulb filament while fishing, and Wyoming women's history. Allow 30-45 minutes.

map_keywords: Sinks Canyon State Park Lander Wyoming
Wild Iris climbing area Lander Wyoming

visits.text: Six miles southwest of Lander on WY-131, the Middle Fork of the Popo Agie River pulls one of geology's stranger tricks: it disappears into a limestone cavern called 'the Sinks' and reappears a quarter mile downstream at 'the Rise' - taking more than two hours underground to cover that quarter mile. A paved path connects the two sites. At the Rise, a pool holds 8-12 pound rainbow trout protected from fishing since 1919; toss in food and the water churns. Humans have used this canyon for approximately 9,000 years. Free admission.

The Fremont County Pioneer Museum in downtown Lander anchors the town's founding logic: Lander was named for Frederick W. Lander, engineer of the Lander Trail used by thousands of westward emigrants. When the Chicago & North Western Transportation Company chose Lander as the western terminus of its 'Cowboy Line' railway in 1906, the phrase stuck - 'where the rails end and the trails begin.' It still fits today. Free admission.

Twenty-five miles south of Lander, Wild Iris is one of the best sport-climbing areas in North America - over 300 routes in bone-white dolomite limestone surrounded by aspen groves and wildflower meadows. Todd Skinner and partners first developed it in 1990. Even without climbing shoes, the drive alone is worth it: the meadows are spectacular in June and July, and Lander's International Climbers' Festival, running every July since 1994, is the longest-running climbing festival in the United States.

activities.text: The Canyon Loop Trail in Sinks Canyon crosses the river on a swinging bridge, loops past the Sinks and the Rise, then climbs the north wall for views across the limestone cliffs. The rock type shifts from sandstone to limestone to granite within a few miles as the Wind River Range closes in. Watch for bighorn sheep on the upper cliffs, mule deer in the canyon bottom, and moose near the river in early morning.

practical_info.tip: Morning light on the canyon walls is best for photography

activities.text: Main Street Lander in the evening has a working Western character unusual for a town of 7,500. Wild Iris Mountain Sports at 425 Popo Agie St is worth stopping in - staff have climbed every crag in the region and give genuine advice on the Wind Rivers. For dinner, Lander Brewing Company serves local craft beer alongside bison dishes; the Gannett Grill is the other local anchor.

map_keywords: Hot Springs State Park Thermopolis Wyoming
Wind River Canyon Scenic Byway Wyoming

visits.text: Hot Springs State Park smells of minerals from the parking lot. The Big Spring - Wyoming's first state park since 1897 - discharges more than 1.8 million gallons of water per day at a constant 128°F, flowing over rainbow-colored travertine terraces in layers of orange, white, and rust. The free public Bath House at the park's core is a condition of a treaty signed that year with the Eastern Shoshone and Northern Arapaho, who sold the land on the condition the waters remain free. The Bath House, cooled to 104°F, limits soaks to 20 minutes. Cross the suspension footbridge over the Bighorn River for the best view of the terraces.

In 1993, fossil hunters found dinosaur bones weathering out of the hills on Warm Springs Ranch ten minutes from downtown Thermopolis. The Wyoming Dinosaur Center grew from that discovery and now houses 58 mounted skeletons and over 20,000 bones from the Thermopolis bonebeds - including the Thermopolis Specimen of Archaeopteryx, one of the most complete ever found. Watch scientists at work in the preparation lab year-round; the Dig for the Day program (late May through mid-September) puts you on a real excavation site with actual paleontology tools. Book in advance.

Four miles south on US-20, Wind River Canyon closes around the road - sheer walls, the Wind River running alongside, rock layers exposing more than 2.5 billion years of geological history in one of the most complete sequences visible anywhere in Wyoming. Keep your eyes on the upper ledges for bighorn sheep. The canyon runs about 14 miles; allow 20-30 minutes with stops.

activities.text: For a longer soak than the 20-minute Bath House limit allows, Star Plunge or Hellie's Tepee Spa charge paid entry but offer waterslides, multiple pools, and no time restrictions - both fed by the same mineral waters. Star Plunge has been operating since 1900. The State Bath House allows a second 20-minute session if you wait two hours between them.

practical_info.admission: paid entry for commercial pools; state bath house free

activities.text: Twenty-one miles northwest of Thermopolis, the Legend Rock Petroglyph Site carries the work of three prehistoric cultural groups on sandstone cliffs spanning roughly 500 AD through the 19th century - hundreds of yards of images. Pick up the gate key at Hot Springs State Park headquarters before leaving town; free to visit. The road turns to gravel for the last few miles. Bring water: it's exposed high desert.

practical_info.key: Pick up gate key at Hot Springs State Park headquarters before leaving Thermopolis

map_keywords: Buffalo Bill Center of the West Cody Wyoming

visits.text: Five museums share one roof at the Buffalo Bill Center of the West, 720 Sheridan Avenue - a Smithsonian Affiliate since 2008 and the oldest museum complex dedicated to the American West, founded in 1917. The five: Buffalo Bill Museum (Wild West show artifacts and personal effects), Cody Firearms Museum (7,000 individual firearms, the most comprehensive collection in the US), Whitney Western Art Museum (Remington, Russell), Draper Natural History Museum (Yellowstone geology and wildlife), and Plains Indian Museum (Arapaho, Lakota, Crow and Cheyenne objects; first curator was George Horse Capture of the A'aninin tribe). Your ticket is valid for two consecutive days.

Old Trail Town on the western edge of Cody is 26 historic frontier buildings gathered from original sites across Wyoming by archaeologist Bob Edgar, starting in 1967. The collection includes a fur traders' cabin from 1883, a dance hall, a saloon, and the grave of mountain man John 'Liver-Eating' Johnston. One of the few places in the West where the structures are original, not reconstructed. Paid entry; open May through September.

The Irma Hotel on Sheridan Avenue opened in November 1902 - Buffalo Bill Cody's own hotel, built for $80,000 and named after his youngest daughter. At the center of the dining room sits a cherrywood back bar said to have been a gift from Queen Victoria in recognition of a Wild West show command performance at her court; the hotel's current owner acknowledges there is no documentary proof, but the bar has been here since opening day. Walk in for a drink or a meal without booking; the Cody Gunfighters do a free shootout on the front sidewalk at 6 p.m. nightly in summer.

activities.text: Attend the Cody Nite Rodeo at Stampede Park - every single night from June 1 through August 31 since 1938, making Cody the only town in the United States with a nightly rodeo. Former Wild West show performer Carly Downing founded it, originally calling it the 'Pup Rodeo.' The 6,000-seat arena sits on a terrace above the Shoshone River west of downtown; gates open at 7:00 p.m., rodeo starts at 8:00 p.m. A bus departs from most hotels for ~$7 round-trip. Paid entry; book ahead in peak summer.

practical_info.tip: Rodeo bus from hotels ~$7 round-trip, kids under 10 free on bus

activities.text: West of Cody, US-14/16/20 follows the North Fork of the Shoshone River through 52 miles of Shoshone National Forest that Theodore Roosevelt called 'the most scenic 50 miles in the United States.' Dramatic volcanic formations line both sides. No Yellowstone entrance fee applies if you turn around at the East Gate - back in Cody in 2-3 hours.

practical_info.tip: No Yellowstone entrance fee unless you drive past the East Entrance gate

to_next_leg.road_type: mountain pass (US-14A Medicine Wheel Passage, grades exceeding 10%, max elevation 9,430 ft - not suitable for trailers or large motorhomes)

map_keywords: Trail End State Historic Site Sheridan

visits.text: Forest Road 12 off US-14A leads to a lower parking lot, from which a 1.5-mile gravel road climbs to the Medicine Wheel: white limestone boulders arranged in a circle 82 feet across, 28 radial spokes extending from a central cairn, six outer cairns at the rim. All of this sits at 9,642 feet and has been visited by Native Americans for at least 7,000 years. Designated a National Historic Landmark in 1970, it remains an active ceremonial site used by tribes from across the Northern Plains - negotiations over the 1996 Historic Preservation Plan involved representatives of 16 tribes. If a ceremony is underway when you arrive, stand back and observe quietly. Access road opens July 1.

King's Saddlery at 184 N. Main Street is a working shop, not a museum - leather smell, power tools, artisans building saddles and ropes. Don King opened it in 1963 after learning his trade riding cattle trails through Texas, Arizona and California as a child; his custom saddles are in the Cowboy Hall of Fame and the ProRodeo Hall of Champions. Behind the shop is the Don King Museum - free to enter - with historic saddles, wagons, photographs, and leather-working tools spanning a century of Western craft. Ask the staff to watch them work.

The Historic Sheridan Inn at 856 Broadway Street opened in 1893 - built by the Chicago, Burlington and Quincy Railroad and designed by Thomas Kimball after a Scottish hunting lodge. It was the first building in the area with electric lights and bathtubs, and was said at opening to be the finest hotel between Chicago and San Francisco. Buffalo Bill Cody was a part-owner who auditioned Wild West show performers from the wraparound front porch. Reopened in 2015 after a 50-year closure; walk in for a meal or a drink without staying.

activities.text: The descent into Sheridan via the Bighorn Scenic Byway (US-14) from Burgess Junction to Dayton offers two stops worth pulling over for: Shell Falls, where water plunges 120 feet over granite cliffs with an interpretive walkway, and Steamboat Point, where a steep single-track trail reaches a 360-degree viewpoint over the valley. The full run is 58 miles; allow 90 minutes. US-14 handles all vehicle sizes - US-14A does not.

practical_info.warning: US-14A (grades 10%+) is not recommended for trailers; US-14 is the safer alternative for large vehicles

activities.text: Sheridan's Main Street in the evening is worth lingering in - the 1923 WYO Theater at 42 N. Main still hosts live performances in a building that newspapers called 'Wyoming's Wonder Picture Palace' when it opened. A few blocks away, the Mint Bar has been a gathering spot since 1907: ranchers, rodeo cowboys, and passing travelers on the same barstools. No performance for tourists; just a working Western town doing what it does.

map_keywords: National Historic Trails Interpretive Center Casper Wyoming

visits.text: Pull off I-25 for the National Historic Trails Interpretive Center - free admission, and one of Wyoming's better history museums. Casper sits at the crossing of four western migration corridors: Oregon, California, Mormon Pioneer, and Pony Express trails. The museum covers the 400,000 emigrants who crossed this ground between 1841 and 1868, with hands-on exhibits including a virtual covered wagon ride and a treadmill that simulates pulling a handcart. A 27-minute film addresses US treaty violations with Plains tribes. Allow 75-90 minutes.

Fort Caspar Museum sits on the banks of the North Platte River in a reconstructed 1865 military post - named for 2nd Lieutenant Caspar Collins, killed at the Battle of Platte Bridge Station that year. A typo in the 1889 town registration swapped the 'a' for an 'e' in Casper, which is why the town and the fort are spelled differently. The museum covers prehistoric Plains Indians, the frontier army, and the energy industry. Open daily May through September.

map_keywords: Quebec 01 Missile Alert Facility Cheyenne Wyoming
Nelson Museum of the West Cheyenne

visits.text: Thirty minutes north of downtown, the Quebec 01 Missile Alert Facility State Historic Site is the only publicly accessible Peacekeeper Missile Alert Facility remaining in the world. Topside is a self-guided museum of Cold War daily life; underground guided tours take you through the actual launch control room - codes, lockboxes, escape hatches designed to fill with sand after a nuclear blast. The 90th Missile Wing at F.E. Warren Air Force Base still operates Minuteman III missiles today. Free topside; paid entry for the underground tour.

The Wyoming Historic Governors' Mansion at 300 East 21st Street is a Colonial Revival building from 1904 that housed 19 Wyoming First Families - among them Nellie Tayloe Ross, the first female governor in United States history, elected in 1924. Docent-led tours move through rooms decorated to represent different eras of occupancy. Open Tuesday through Saturday; free admission.

The Nelson Museum of the West downtown holds one of the larger collections of saddles, carriages, and Old West memorabilia in the region - cowboys, lawmen, Native Americans, and soldiers across the frontier period. Free admission for most of the year. Allow 60 minutes and combine with a walk through the historic district nearby.

activities.text: The Cheyenne Street Railway Trolley runs a 90-minute narrated loop of the historic district - railroad quarter, Capitol Hill, historic mansions, Fort D.A. Russell. Quickest way to orient yourself before deciding which buildings to revisit on foot. Runs in summer only; paid entry; departs from Depot Plaza.

Close out the loop at Westby Edge Brewing downtown - local craft beer, the same city where the itinerary started nine days ago. Denver is 90 minutes south on I-25; factor that into your checkout time and don't book a departure before noon.

seo.h1_title: Wyoming Beyond Yellowstone: Cowboys, Hot Springs & Bighorn Medicine Wheel
seo.intro_paragraph: This 8-day loop from Denver covers the Wyoming that road-trippers drive straight through: a mineral hot spring in Thermopolis free to soak since an 1897 treaty, a nightly rodeo in Cody running every summer since 1938, and a 7,000-year-old sacred stone wheel at 9,642 feet in the Bighorn Mountains. The loop threads south Wyoming's mountain passes and high-plains fishing towns before climbing north through dinosaur country and back down through Sheridan's intact 1893 Main Street.
seo.seo_hook: Wyoming's real West in 8 days: free hot springs, a nightly rodeo, the Medicine Wheel, and Butch Cassidy's prison cell.
featured_list: Thermopolis: Hot Springs State Park - Big Spring discharges 1.8 million gallons per day at 128°F; free public bathing since an 1897 treaty with the Shoshone and Arapaho
Cody: Cody Nite Rodeo - nightly June through August since 1938, the only town in the US with a rodeo every night of the summer
Bighorn Mountains: Medicine Wheel - 82-foot-diameter stone structure at 9,642 feet, active sacred site for more than 80 tribes, National Historic Landmark since 1970
Sheridan: Sheridan Inn - built 1893, first hotel in the area with electric lights and bathtubs, Buffalo Bill auditioned Wild West show acts from the front porch

places_taglines.US::cheyenne: Wyoming's capital since 1867, where the gold-domed Capitol and the oldest outdoor rodeo in the country share the same skyline.
places_taglines.US::laramie: Butch Cassidy's 1872 sandstone prison and 1.4 billion-year-old granite formations 16 miles down the interstate.
places_taglines.US::saratoga: Free 24-hour mineral hot spring in the city park, and blue-ribbon trout at 4,000 fish per mile on the North Platte.
places_taglines.US::rawlins: Wyoming's first state penitentiary ran for 80 years; its museum holds shoes made from a hanged outlaw's skin.
places_taglines.US::lander: Where the rails ended and the trails began - gateway to the Wind Rivers, 2,500 climbing routes, and the US's longest-running climbing festival.
places_taglines.US::thermopolis: The world's largest mineral hot spring by measured discharge, free to soak since 1897, and 58 dinosaur skeletons ten minutes from the springs.
places_taglines.US::cody: Buffalo Bill's own town: five Smithsonian-affiliated museums and a rodeo every night of the summer since 1938.
places_taglines.US::sheridan: A working 1893 saddlemaker, a National Historic Landmark hotel, and the sacred Medicine Wheel 46 miles up in the Bighorns.
places_taglines.US::casper: Four western migration trails crossed the same river ford here - Oregon, California, Mormon Pioneer, and Pony Express.

seo_keywords: Buffalo Bill Center of the West
North Platte River fly fishing Saratoga

highlights: Hot Springs State Park: free public soaking, 1.8 million gallons/day at 128°F, Wyoming's oldest state park since 1897
Cody Nite Rodeo: every night June 1-August 31 since 1938, the only nightly rodeo in the United States
Medicine Wheel NHL: 9,642 ft, 82-foot-diameter stone wheel, active sacred site for 80+ tribes, access road opens July 1
Snowy Range Scenic Byway WY-130: second-highest pass in Wyoming at 10,847 ft, open Memorial Day to mid-October
North Platte River at Saratoga: 4,000 wild trout per mile, unstocked since 1979, free Hobo Hot Pool open 24/7 year-round

itineraries.title: Alabama Civil Rights Trail: Birmingham to Mobile
itineraries.subtitle: From the church that changed America to the city that invented Mardi Gras
itineraries.notes: 6-day linear drive from Birmingham (BHM) to Mobile (MOB). Total driving: ~450 miles. A mandatory intermediate stop at Monroeville breaks the Montgomery-Mobile leg (2h30 without a stop). The Legacy Museum and National Memorial in Montgomery require pre-purchased tickets; both sell out. Brown Chapel tours in Selma and 16th Street Baptist Church tours both require advance booking. This itinerary covers some of the most painful events in American history - plan pacing carefully.

visits.text: The air goes still the moment you step through the door. On September 15, 1963, at 10:22 a.m., KKK members detonated 19 sticks of dynamite under the steps of this three-story Romanesque-Byzantine building - the first Black church in Birmingham, organized in 1873. Four girls died in the basement restroom: Addie Mae Collins (14), Cynthia Wesley (14), Carole Robertson (14), and Denise McNair (11). Her shoes are on display across the street at the Civil Rights Institute. Tours are guided; book ahead at 16thstreetbaptist.org - walk-in capacity is limited.

Four acres, and every square foot is loaded. This is where Bull Connor's police turned fire hoses and attack dogs on children in May 1963 - more than 1,000 students had marched out of the church behind them. James Drake's three steel installations along the Freedom Walk put you between snarling dog sculptures and hose nozzles aimed at eye level. The Four Spirits sculpture, unveiled in September 2013 on the bombing's 50th anniversary, shows four barefooted girls in their Sunday best, one releasing six doves. Free entry, open daily.

The building's ramp slopes upward the entire way through - that's intentional, a physical metaphor the designers built into the floor plan. Inside the Birmingham Civil Rights National Monument (proclaimed January 2017 by President Obama), the museum holds the actual door from the jail where King wrote his 'Letter from a Birmingham Jail,' the personal effects found on Denise McNair's body, and a full-scale Freedom Rider bus. Budget two hours minimum. Closed Mondays; paid admission.

Built in 1954 by Arthur George Gaston - Birmingham's first Black millionaire - to give Black travelers first-class lodging in a Jim Crow city, the motel appears in the Negro Motorist Green Book and became the nerve center of the 1963 Birmingham Campaign. Room 30, nicknamed the 'War Room,' is where King, Abernathy, and Shuttlesworth met to plan Project C. On May 11, 1963, a bomb destroyed part of the building. Today co-owned by the National Park Service and the City of Birmingham, Room 30 is restored to its 1963 appearance. Open Thursday-Saturday, 10am-4pm; free admission.

activities.text: The Birmingham Civil Rights Heritage Trail is a self-guided street route marked by interpretive signs connecting the Civil Rights District to the 4th Avenue Business District - the economic center of Black life in segregated Birmingham, where banks, barbershops, and theaters served a community shut out of the white commercial strip. The full loop is about 1.5 miles on flat pavement. Pick up a map at the Civil Rights Institute before setting out.

practical_info.tip: Maps at the Birmingham Civil Rights Institute reception

activities.text: Five Points South, walking distance from the civil rights sites, has Birmingham's most varied restaurant strip. Highlands Bar & Grill has won James Beard awards - reserve well ahead. For something quicker and just as local, The Essential and El Barrio are within a few blocks. Cahaba Brewing and Avondale Brewing both have taprooms if you want to end the day with something cold and Alabama-made.

visits.text: Sixty-foot blast furnaces, 900 tons of iron produced daily from 1882 to 1971, a 15-acre site that once ranked among the world's largest pig iron producers - Sloss Furnaces is the industrial skeleton Birmingham was built around. Colonel James Withers Sloss broke ground here in 1881. The workforce was racially segregated from the beginning: Black workers drew the most dangerous and physically brutal assignments. Birmingham voters passed a $3.3 million bond in 1977 to save the site from demolition. National Historic Landmark since 1981. Free admission; every structure is walkable.

A 56-foot, 60-ton cast-iron man stands on a pedestal 600 feet above Birmingham - hammer in one hand, spear point raised in the other. Sculptor Giuseppe Moretti built Vulcan in just 40 days in 1904 for the St. Louis World's Fair, where it won a grand prize. The statue is cast from Red Mountain ore, pig iron made right at Sloss Furnaces below. At the time it was the largest statue in the United States, second in height only to the Statue of Liberty. The observation crown elevator gives you the fastest read of Birmingham's geography - blast furnaces to the north, suburbs in every direction. Paid entry to the tower.

During segregation, the 4th Avenue Historic District was the economic and social center of Black life in Birmingham - doctors, lawyers, entrepreneurs, and entertainers who were locked out of the white commercial strip built their own strip here. The two- and three-story brick buildings date from the 1890s to the 1940s; the Carver Theatre (1935) now houses the Alabama Jazz Hall of Fame. A short loop by car or on foot; exterior viewing is free.

activities.text: Red Mountain Park opened in 2012 on 1,500 acres of former Ishkooda mine land, where iron ore was strip-mined from the late 1800s through the mid-20th century. The trails pass open ore pits, rusting ore cars, and the physical wreckage of the extraction industry before cresting the ridge for city views. The Theiss Treetop Experience canopy walk runs above the mining ruins. Easy to moderate terrain; free admission. Download the trail map from redmountainpark.org before going.

practical_info.tip: Download the trail map from redmountainpark.org before you go

activities.text: Sloss Furnaces runs iron casting and metalworking classes open to the public - the only blast furnace site in the country where you can actually make something from iron on the premises. Schedules vary; check slossfurnaces.org for open workshop dates. On most spring and fall weekends, the large cast shed doubles as a concert venue. Ask the visitor center what's on the schedule.

visits.text: Stand at the west end and look across. The Edmund Pettus Bridge arches high enough that from the starting point you can't see what's on the other side - the 600 marchers on March 7, 1965 had no idea what waited for them until they reached the crown. Then they saw it: 150 state troopers and possemen, some on horseback, stretched across Route 80. They were given one minute's warning, then charged. John Lewis suffered a skull fracture. Amelia Boynton was beaten unconscious; the photograph of her lying on the roadway ran on front pages worldwide. The bridge is 1,248 feet long. The name across the crossbeam belongs to Edmund Pettus, Confederate general and Grand Dragon of the Alabama KKK. National Historic Landmark since 2013. Always open, always free.

Six blocks from the bridge, the twin Romanesque towers of Brown Chapel AME Church rise above the surrounding houses. This is where the congregation organized in 1866, one year after Emancipation - the 1908 building by Black builder A.J. Farley is the only surviving work he's known to have built. Martin Luther King Jr. spoke from this pulpit on January 2, 1965, urging registration. On March 7, 600 marchers gathered on these steps and walked two-by-two through downtown. Brown Chapel provided food, shelter, and organizing space throughout the entire 1965 campaign. National Historic Landmark 1997. Tours by appointment: (334) 874-7897.

The National Park Service's free Selma Interpretive Center, blocks from the bridge, puts the full year of 1965 in context - because Bloody Sunday was the visible moment of a campaign that had been running since 1963. In Dallas County that year, Black citizens made up more than half the population but just 2 percent of registered voters. Rangers explain the voter registration drives, the violence, and the three marches. The center marks the official start of the Selma to Montgomery National Historic Trail, a 54-mile federally designated route following the 1965 march. Free admission; the 25-minute ranger film is worth watching.

activities.text: The Selma Civil Rights Heritage Trail links the bridge, Brown Chapel, the Old Depot Museum, and the National Voting Rights Museum on Water Avenue - all on foot, about a mile, flat ground. At the foot of the bridge, the Voting Rights Museum holds oral histories from marchers who were present on Bloody Sunday. The signage on the trail is clear; no guide is needed. Small admission fee at the Voting Rights Museum.

practical_info.admission: Small fee at the Voting Rights Museum
practical_info.tip: Signage on the trail is clear; no guide needed

activities.text: Drive east on US-80 to the Lowndes County Interpretive Center, a free NPS site about 20 miles out on the historic march route. Lowndes County is where marchers camped in open fields on Day 3 of the third march under National Guard protection, and where Viola Liuzzo - a white civil rights worker from Detroit - was shot and killed by KKK members on March 25, 1965, as she drove marchers back to Selma after the march reached Montgomery. The center tells that story without softening it. Free admission.

practical_info.distance: About 20 miles east of Selma on US-80

map_keywords: Dexter Avenue King Memorial Baptist Church

visits.text: The museum stands on the exact site of the old Empire Theatre bus stop on Cleveland Avenue - the corner where Rosa Parks was arrested on December 1, 1955, for refusing to give up her seat on Bus #2857. Opened on December 1, 2000, the 45th anniversary of the arrest, the Rosa Parks Museum holds her original fingerprint arrest record, a 1950s Montgomery city bus, court documents, and the restored 1955 station wagon used as a 'rolling church' for the 381-day boycott carpool. The Bus Room reenactment runs every ten minutes. Open Monday-Saturday; paid admission.

Four blocks from the museum, the red-brick Dexter Avenue King Memorial Baptist Church sits directly under the shadow of the State Capitol - a proximity that was never accidental. The congregation organized in 1877 in a former slave trader's pen on what was then Market Street; the current building was constructed between 1883 and 1889. Martin Luther King Jr. arrived as pastor in 1954 at age 25 and ran the 381-day Bus Boycott from the basement office. Designated a National Historic Landmark in 1974; on UNESCO's Tentative World Heritage List since 2008. Hourly guided tours Tuesday-Saturday, 10am-3pm.

At the top of Dexter Avenue, a plaque marks where Jefferson Davis was inaugurated president of the Confederacy on February 18, 1861. A few feet away, a star marks where Martin Luther King Jr. ended the Selma-to-Montgomery march on March 25, 1965, speaking to 25,000 people on these same steps. The Alabama State Capitol has sat here continuously since 1846. Court Square at the base of the avenue was an active slave auction site into the Civil War. Free exterior access; interior tours available.

activities.text: Maya Lin designed the Civil Rights Memorial on Washington Avenue - a black granite disc, water flowing in a thin sheet over the names of 40 people killed in the movement between 1955 and 1968, dedicated in 1989 by the Southern Poverty Law Center. Two blocks away, sculptor Clydetta Fulmer's bronze Rosa Parks statue was unveiled on December 1, 2019 - 64 years after the arrest - with the State Capitol visible over her shoulder. Both are free, open air, and within easy walking distance of the church and museum.

Chris' Famous Hot Dogs at 138 Dexter Avenue has been here since 1917, two blocks from the Rosa Parks arrest site, and has fed three U.S. presidents and every Alabama governor on record. It's the kind of place that takes ten minutes and feels exactly right after a full day of heavy history. Old Alabama Town, a six-acre restored complex of 19th-century structures nearby, is open until 4pm for those who want more texture of the era.

map_keywords: National Memorial for Peace and Justice Montgomery

visits.text: The Legacy Museum stands on Montgomery Street on land where enslaved people were warehoused between auction dates. Between 1850 and 1865, Montgomery was one of the largest slave-trading centers in the United States. Opened April 26, 2018 by the Equal Justice Initiative, the museum moves through 400 years: the transatlantic slave trade, Reconstruction, the era of racial terror and lynching, Jim Crow, and the mass incarceration crisis Stevenson documented in his 2014 book Just Mercy. First-person accounts play from glass enclosures recreating a holding cell. The art collection includes works by Jacob Lawrence, Glenn Ligon, and Kwame Akoto-Bamfo. Paid admission; book ahead at legacysites.eji.org.

The hill above downtown Montgomery holds 810 corten steel monuments - one for every US county where a racial terror lynching was documented - hanging from the ceiling of the National Memorial for Peace and Justice, opened April 26, 2018. More than 4,400 Black Americans were killed in racial terror lynchings between 1877 and 1950; this is the first comprehensive memorial to them. The pathway drops as you walk through the central structure until the monuments tower above your head. Outside, duplicate monuments lie flat on the ground, waiting to be claimed and installed by their counties. Six acres, designed by MASS Design Group. Paid admission, same entry bundle as the Legacy Museum.

A baby blue 1952 Cadillac convertible sits in the center of the Hank Williams Museum on Commerce Street - the car he was riding in when he died on January 1, 1953, at 29, somewhere between Knoxville and Canton, Ohio. Williams was born in Georgiana in 1923, learned guitar from a Black street musician named Rufus 'Tee-Tot' Payne on Montgomery street corners, and had the number-one country record in America by 1949. The museum holds his handwritten lyrics, stage costumes, and the artifacts of a career that lasted seven years and defined a genre. Paid admission; 45 minutes is enough.

activities.text: The Freedom Monument Sculpture Park, a 17-acre EJI site opened in 2022 on the Alabama River banks, explores the lived experience of enslaved people in Montgomery through art and landscape. Dana King's 'Guided by Justice' - three women, a grandmother, a teacher, and a pregnant woman, their footprints pressed into the pavement - stands near the water as an explicit call to continue. Free admission; a shuttle connects the park with the Legacy Museum and Memorial.

practical_info.tip: Free EJI shuttle connects Legacy Museum, Memorial, and Sculpture Park

activities.text: Before leaving Montgomery, walk all six blocks of Dexter Avenue one last time. Court Square at the base was an antebellum slave auction site. Midway up, the Winter Building is where the telegraph ordering the Confederate attack on Fort Sumter was sent on April 11, 1861. Above it sits King's church. At the top, the Capitol steps where both a Confederacy and a civil rights march ended. Twenty minutes on foot; more concentrated American history per block than almost anywhere in the country.

visits.text: The Old Monroe County Courthouse was built in 1903 at a cost that nearly bankrupted Monroe County. Harper Lee watched her father, attorney A.C. Lee, try cases from the balcony upstairs throughout her childhood. When Hollywood came to film To Kill a Mockingbird in 1962, the courtroom was still an active court. Art director Henry Bumstead flew to Monroeville, photographed and measured every detail, and rebuilt the entire room on a California soundstage - winning an Academy Award for it. The exhibits cover Lee's life and her friendship with Capote, who moved next door in 1930 at age six. Paid admission. A stage production of the novel runs in this very courtroom every April and May; tickets at tokillamockingbird.com sell out months in advance.

map_keywords: USS Alabama Battleship Memorial Park Mobile

visits.text: The USS Alabama rises from Mobile Bay at 680 feet long, 45,000 tons fully loaded - a South Dakota-class battleship commissioned August 16, 1942, and known to her 2,500-man crew as the 'Mighty A.' She deployed to the Atlantic in 1943, then the Pacific, earning nine Battle Stars. On September 5, 1945, she led the American fleet into Tokyo Bay. She voyaged 218,000 miles during the war without losing a single man to enemy fire. In 1963, Alabama schoolchildren contributed $100,000 in nickels and dimes to save her from the scrap heap. Open to visitors since January 9, 1965. The full self-tour includes the submarine USS Drum below the waterline. Paid admission.

The Cathedral Basilica of the Immaculate Conception on Dauphin Street belongs to the oldest Catholic parish on the Gulf Coast - established in 1703, the same year Mobile was founded and Mardi Gras first celebrated. The current Gothic Revival structure was built between 1835 and 1850; Pope John XXIII designated it a minor basilica in 1962. The stained glass panels were imported from Germany in the early 1900s and took a decade to complete. A spiral staircase leads down to the underground crypt where past archbishops are interred. Open for self-guided visits outside Mass hours; free admission.

Climb aboard a full-sized rocking float in the former carriage house - the best possible way to understand what it feels like to be masked and throwing Moon Pies to 50,000 people in the street below. The Mobile Carnival Museum, opened in 2005 inside the restored 1872 Bernstein-Bush mansion on Government Street, chronicles over 300 years of Carnival across 14 galleries. Mobile's first Mardi Gras was celebrated in 1703, started by Frenchman Nicholas Langlois when the city was capital of French Louisiana - two decades before New Orleans held its first celebration. The Queen's Gallery holds gowns and jewels from over a century of Carnival royalty. Paid admission.

activities.text: Dauphin Street has been Mobile's main commercial artery since the French laid it out in the early 1700s. The stretch between Broad Street and the waterfront holds restaurants, bars, and shops in 19th-century brick buildings. Wintzell's Oyster House at 605 Dauphin has been shucking since 1938. Walk toward the river end for views over the port and the USS Alabama visible across the bay.
